abstract: The Brønsted acidity of proton-exchanged zeolites remains the cornerstone of their industrial success, driving pivotal transformations of hydrocarbons and oxygenates in heterogeneous catalysis. Over several decades, extensive research aimed at unraveling these catalytic mechanisms at the atomic scale has fundamentally reshaped our understanding of how acidity and spatial confinement synergistically dictate reactivity. These evolving concepts now represent a crucial intersection between heterogeneous catalysis and molecular chemistry. This review provides a comprehensive molecular perspective on the mechanisms governing generic transformations at zeolitic Brønsted acid sites, integrating insights from advanced kinetic analysis, in situ and operando spectroscopy, and quantum chemical modeling. We first evaluate the current understanding of the nature of Brønsted acid sites and the governing parameters of zeolitic activity, followed by an in-depth focus on the reactivity of alkenes, alkanes, aromatics, alcohols, and polyhydroxy compounds. Central to these discussions are the elementary steps of C–C, C–H, and C–O bond activation and formation. Finally, we offer an outlook on overcoming future challenges in the field, with the ultimate ambition of establishing a rational framework for the design of next-generation Brønsted acid catalysts.
